Step-by-Step Instructions

6.1 Setup

Setting Up Data Relationships

Connecting your actual spending data to the budget table through a shared key unlocks variance analysis — you can compare what was spent vs. what was planned without ever merging data manually.

1. 6.1.1 Load the Budget file: Home → Get Data → Excel workbook
6.1.2 Navigate to Budget Data → click Open
6.1.3 Tick Budget in the Navigator → click Transform Data
2. 6.1.4 Review the Budget query in Power Query Editor → click Close & Apply
6.1.5 Create a UniqueKey column to link both tables:
• Table View → Budget table → Column Tools → New Column
UniqueKey = Budget[Project]&Budget[Material]&Budget[Date]
• Table View → Data table → Column Tools → New Column
UniqueKey = Data[Project]&Data[Material]&Data[Date]
3. 6.1.6 Switch to Model View → drag UniqueKey from the Budget table and drop it onto UniqueKey in the Data table to create the relationship
6.1.7 Click Save (Ctrl+S)
4. 6.1.8 Switch to Report View → create a new page named "Actual and Budget" → add a Matrix Visual with:
• Rows: Project, Material (Data table)
• Columns: Date Year (Data table)
• Values: Sum of Total Cost (Data table), Sum of Budgeted Cost (Budget table)
💡The UniqueKey concatenates Project + Material + Date — this composite key ensures each row in both tables matches exactly, enabling accurate budget-vs-actual comparisons across all dimensions.
6.2 DAX

Using DAX for Performance Appraisal

DAX measures let you calculate Variance, Year-on-Year growth, and budget adherence dynamically — they respond to slicers and filters automatically, making your report truly interactive.

1. 6.2.1 Create a new page named "DAX" → add a Slicer → drag Date Year (Data table) into Field
Format the slicer: Visualizations → Format Visual → Slicer settings → Options → Style: Vertical list
2. 6.2.2 Click 2024 in the Year slicer to focus on the latest period
Create a Matrix visual: Rows → Project, Material (Data table) — this will be the scaffold for all DAX measures
3. 6.2.3 Create the Actual Cost measure:
In the Data pane, hover over the Data table → click … → New Measure → enter:
Actual Cost = SUM(Data[Total Cost])
Drag Actual Cost into the Matrix Values field
4. 6.2.4 Create the Budgeted Cost measure — New Measure on the Data table:
Budgeted Cost = SUM(Budget[Budgeted Cost])
Drag Budgeted Cost into the Matrix Values field
5. 6.2.5 Create the Variance measure:
Variance = [Budgeted Cost]-[Actual Cost]
Drag Variance into the Matrix Values
6.2.6 Create the Variance% measure:
Variance% = DIVIDE([Variance],[Budgeted Cost])
Drag Variance% into the Matrix Values → select the measure → Measure Tools → Format → Percentage (%)
6. 6.2.7 Create the Actual Cost – Previous Year measure:
Actual Cost - Previous Year = CALCULATE([Actual Cost],SAMEPERIODLASTYEAR(Data[Date].[Date]))
Drag into the Matrix
6.2.8 Create the Actual Cost – YoY% measure:
Actual Cost - YoY% = DIVIDE([Actual Cost]-[Actual Cost - Previous Year],[Actual Cost - Previous Year])
Drag into the Matrix → format as Percentage (%)
💡Build DAX measures in order — Actual Cost and Budgeted Cost first, then Variance (which references them), then Variance% (which references Variance). This dependency chain ensures each measure builds correctly on the one before it.
6.3 Insights

Communicating Data Insights

Raw numbers in a matrix don't tell a story — this page transforms your DAX measures into a visual narrative: KPI cards for at-a-glance status, a scatter chart to spot outliers, and a trend line to see where costs are heading.

1. 6.3.1 Create a new page named "Executive Overview_Rebuild" → add a Slicer → drag Date Year (Data table) into Field
Format: Visualizations → Format Visual → Slicer settings → Options → Style: Vertical list
2. 6.3.2 Click 2024 in the slicer → add a Card (New) visual → drag these measures into the Data field:
Actual Cost, Budgeted Cost, Variance, Variance%, Actual Cost – YoY%
Format: Visualizations → Format → Visual → Layout → Max cards shown: 10 → Space between cards: 2px
3. 6.3.2 (cont.) Increase the callout value size:
Visualizations → Format → Visual → Callout values → Values → 20
4. 6.3.3 Create a Scatter Chart with:
• Values: Project, Material | X-Axis: Variance% | Y-Axis: Actual Cost – YoY% | Size: Actual Cost
Turn on category labels: Format → Visual → Category label → toggle ON
5. 6.3.3 (cont.) Resize the markers for better visibility:
Visualizations → Format → Visual → Markers → Shape → Size multiplier: –20
6. 6.3.4 Create a Line and Clustered Column Chart:
• X-Axis: Date Month | Column Y-Axis: Actual Cost – YoY% | Line Y-Axis: Variance%
Add a title: Format → General → Title → Text: "Cost Variance% and YoY% Trend"
7. 6.3.5 Create a summary Matrix Visual with:
• Rows: Project, Material
• Values: Actual Cost, Budgeted Cost, Variance, Variance%, Actual Cost – YoY%
This table lets executives drill into the numbers behind the visuals above
💡The scatter chart is the most powerful visual on this page — bubbles in the top-right quadrant (high Variance%, high YoY%) are your best performers. Bottom-left means over budget AND declining. Size = Actual Cost tells you which of those quadrants matters most financially.

Module 6 Recap

You've connected two data sources through a UniqueKey relationship, built a full suite of DAX performance measures — Actual Cost, Budgeted Cost, Variance, Variance%, and Year-on-Year — and assembled an executive dashboard with KPI cards, a scatter chart, a combo trend chart, and a summary matrix.

Combined with Modules 4 and 5, you now have an end-to-end Power BI pipeline: raw data → cleaned & transformed → explored & reported → analysed & communicated to stakeholders.

The scatter chart quadrant analysis and YoY% trends give leadership exactly what they need to identify cost overruns, track improvement, and make data-driven decisions.

🎉 All 3 sections completed! Your executive performance dashboard is ready to present.
